segments.s175-g65: چو is the subordinating particle 'when'. رعد بهاران is a genitival phrase 'the thunder of spring' (rational word order: noun + modifier). بغرید is a past tense/reporting form 'roared' applied to رعد, and گیو (a proper name) follows as the subject of the next clause (Ferdowsi often strings clauses together without a conjunction).
segments.s175-g66: ز here functions as a preposition meaning 'from' (poetic use). سالار لشکر is a noun‑phrase 'the commander of the army' (yearly order: modifier follows core noun). همی is the old continuative particle (indicating ongoing or habitual action), جست is the classical past infinitive/preterite 'leapt' (literal 'he leapt'), and نیو is a brief poetic simile/adverb (here used as an image; Ferdowsi uses short nouns as adverbial comparisons).
segments.s175-g67: چو introduces a temporal subordinate clause 'when'. بشنید is the past verb 'he heard'. پیرانش is پیران (elders) with the 3rd‑person enclitic -ش 'his' (his elders). دشنام داد is a common compound 'gave abuse' = 'shouted insults/ cursed'. Word order: subject (پیرانش) follows the verb here because of poetic inversion.
segments.s175-g68: بدو گفت is a light verb construction: بدو 'to him' (indirect object/enclitic with dative sense) + گفت 'said' (here used as the reporting verb). کای is the vocative 'O' (address marker). بد رگ is a two‑word phrase: بد 'bad/ill' modifies رگ, where رگ is used figuratively = 'disposition/temper' (so 'ill‑disposed' or 'of bad blood'). دیوزاد is a compound noun 'demon‑born' or 'fiend‑born'. The clause is direct speech introduced by the elder's words.
segments.s175-g69: چو 'when' begins the clause. تنها is the adverb 'alone'. The surface 'ب' + 'دین' here functions together as the prepositional phrase 'به + این' = 'to/into this' (Ferdowsi's phrasing sometimes encliticates particles; the glossed lemmas are segmented but read together as 'to this'). رزمگاه is 'battlefield', and آمدی is the archaic/narrative past form used in Ferdowsi for 'came' (in epic Persian this form can mark the third‑person past in narration).
segments.s175-g70: دلاور is the noun 'the brave one/hero'. به پیش is the prepositional phrase 'in front of / before' (به + پیش). سپاه is 'the army/host'. آمدی repeats the same narrative past 'came'—here it describes the hero's movement 'came forward'. Note the inverted/poetic order: the verb appears at the end.
segments.s175-g71: کنون is 'now/at this moment'. خوردنت is the nounified/inflected form of خوردن with enclitic -ت = 'your being struck/your receiving (the blow)' (in epic usage خوردن often means 'to be struck' or 'to receive'). نوک ژوپین is a noun phrase 'the tip/point of the ژوپین' (ژوپین = gambeson/padded jacket). بود is the copula in past narrative sense 'was/would be' used here to state the consequence.
segments.s175-g72: برت is بر + ت, a prepositional phrase 'upon you' (بر = on/upon plus the enclitic pronoun -ت 'you'). را is the definite/object marker emphasizing برت. کفن means 'shroud'. چنگ here is 'claw/talon' rather than the musical instrument (contextual sense). شاهین is 'falcon', and بود again is the past copula 'was/would be', so the line says the falcon's talon would serve as your shroud.
segments.s175-g1: Word order is somewhat inverted: the direct object/group 'سواران' (horsemen) comes before the verbal phrase 'گزین کرد' (selected/did select), and the agent 'پیران' (the elders) follows as the subject; 'هزار' quantifies 'پیران' in position after the verb but really attaches to the elders (a poetic placement). 'کرد' is the past auxiliary of کردن used with گزین to form the past action 'selected'.
segments.s175-g2: This is a verbless appositive clause describing those chosen: 'همه' (all/every one) modifies 'جنگجوی' (warriors), the conjunction 'و' links the second coordinated phrase, and the second 'همه نامدار' repeats the distributive 'all' plus the adjective 'نامدار' (renowned) — a compressed poetic way to say every one of them was a famed warrior.
segments.s175-g3: 'بدیشان' is a prepositional phrase meaning 'to them' (به + ایشان fused), placed before the verb as is common in Persian; 'چنین گفت پیران' literally 'thus said the elders' with the subject 'پیران' following the verb (a poetic/orderly inversion). The conjunction 'که' introduces the content clause that follows; 'زود' is an adverb 'soon/quickly' modifying that clause.
segments.s175-g4: This is the content of the elders' speech. 'عنان' (reins/bridle) is the direct object; 'تگاور' (here 'raider/attacker' or 'swift rider') follows as the noun qualified by or directed at which the reins should be turned; 'بباید' is the old/poetic impersonal/modal form 'it is necessary / you must'; 'بسود' ('to advantage') expresses the purpose or expected benefit. The clause is compressed (typical of epic style) and omits an explicit finite imperative verb: the modal + infinitive sense is implied.
segments.s175-g5: 'شب و روز رفتن' is an infinitival phrase 'to go/march day and night' where the coordinated time adverbial precedes the infinitive 'رفتن'. The simile 'چو شیر ژیان' ('like a lion alive' / 'like a living lion') follows and modifies the manner of going; 'ژیان' is a poetic word for 'alive/living' or 'life' attached to 'شیر'.
segments.s175-g6: 'نباید' is the impersonal negative modal 'one must not / it should not be'; it takes the infinitive 'گشادن' ('to open', 'to break forth', here 'to sally forth/ to open into') as its complement. The prepositional phrase 'به ره بر میان' specifies direction/position: 'به ره' = 'into/on the way/road' and 'بر میان' = 'toward/on the middle'. Together they mean 'into the middle of the road'; the phrase order is compact and poetic.
segments.s175-g7: 'که‌گر' is an older/poetic conditional 'if'; the two names 'گیو و خسرو' are the clause's subjects. The prepositional phrase 'به ایران' indicates movement/toward Iran and the verb 'شوند' is the subjunctive/present plural of شدن used here in a conditional sense 'should be/arrive/go' or simply 'go/become in Iran' (classical Persian uses شدن intransitively in such contexts).
segments.s175-g8: This is the consequence/question clause: 'زنان اندر ایران' (the women in Iran) is the topic, 'چه' is an interrogative/adverb 'what/how' asking for the result, and 'شیران شوند' uses شوند ('become') to ask what they will turn into — literally 'what will the women in Iran become? — (will they) lions?' The construction is rhetorical and anticipatory, a poetic way to question the state of those left behind.
segments.s175-g121: خروش آمد is a simple past verbal clause meaning “a cry/roar arose” (خروش = nominal subject; آمد = past of آمدن). The conjunction و links a second nominal phrase: ناله کرنای is an asyndetic noun phrase “the wail/moan of the karnā (trumpet)” that is presented in continuation rather than with its own finite verb — a common poetic parataxis.
segments.s175-g122: This is a string of instrument‑phrases describing sounds. دم نای = “the blast/breath of the ney (reed‑flute)” where دم is the act/blast and نای names the instrument; رویین modifies نای as an adjective “brazen/iron” (metallic). و links the following phrase هندی درای, where درای is an archaic/poetic form meaning “having/possessing” and هندی qualifies what is possessed (an ‘Indian’ instrument/quality). The whole group is a juxtaposition of sound‑sources rather than a clause with a new verb.
segments.s175-g123: The phrase places the epithet before the name: جهاندیده گیو = “the world‑wise Giv.” اندر آمد is the past tense verbal phrase “entered/came in,” and به آب specifies direction (“into/to the water”); classical Persian often uses اندر + به together for vivid locative/directional expression.
segments.s175-g124: چو introduces a simile (“as/like”), followed by کشتی که … a relative clause introduced by که: که از باد گیرد شتاب = “that takes/gains speed from the wind.” گیرد is the present/habitual form here used in a generalizing simile (“takes”/“gets”); شتاب is the object/noun ‘speed/haste’, and از باد gives the source/agent “from the wind.”
segments.s175-g125: برآورد here is a past transitive verb “raised/lifted” (classical usage; it takes a direct object). گرز گران را is the definite object marked by را (“the heavy mace”), and به کفت is a prepositional phrase indicating where it was placed/held (“into/onto the palm”). Word order is verb + object + locative, typical in older/poetic Persian.
segments.s175-g126: سپه ماند is a past clause “the army remained/stood” (ماند = stayed/was left). از کار او marks the cause/target of their amazement (“because of/having seen his deed”), where از functions as a causal preposition, and در شگفت is a locative/state phrase “in astonishment” (در + شگفت).
segments.s175-g127: This line has two short predicate clauses. سبک شد عنان = “the reins became light/slack” (سبک = light; شد = became; عنان = reins). The following phrase is to be read as و گران شد رکیب (“and the rider became heavy/weighty”); here the conjunction + adjective + شد + subject order is used — a common poetic ordering where the predicate (گران شد) precedes or is joined compactly to its subject رکیب.
segments.s175-g128: سر سرکشان is a genitive/possession phrase meaning “the heads of the (rebellious/)mounted ones” (سر = head; سرکشان = plural/agent phrase — here the group whose heads are affected). خیره گشت = “became stunned/astonished” (خیره as predicate complement + گشت = became), and از نهیب gives the cause “from/because of the shout/thunderous cry.” Note that word order places the affected part (سر) up front for emphasis.
segments.s175-g97: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. The phrase follows Classical Persian word order: subject 'کشتی' (boat) + circumstantial phrase 'ز دشت' (from the plain) + locative 'اندر' + verb 'آمد' (came) and the directional preposition 'به' + goal 'رود' (river). 'اندر آمد به رود' = 'entered into the river.'
segments.s175-g98: همی داد is a habitual/continuous past construction ('was giving' / 'kept giving') where همی marks ongoing action and داد is the past tense verb 'gave'. The object is marked by را: 'نیکی دهش را درود' should be read as 'درود' (greeting) given to 'نیکی دهش' — here a poetic grouping meaning 'the giver/bestower of goodness' (an agentive/epithet); را makes that phrase definite/accusative.
segments.s175-g99: نکرد here is negative past 'did not do' (the contracted negative form of 'نکرده' in poetry); ایچ = 'at all / nothing' intensifies the negation. The following proper name 'گیو' is the logical subject (or topic) and 'آزمون را' is the object 'the trial'. شتاب functions as a noun 'haste' (i.e. 'make haste' — 'did not hasten'). Word order places the negated verb before the emphasized object, typical of poetic inversion.
segments.s175-g100: بدان is an archaic/adverbial form (here 'to that time' / 'until then') followed by the temporal conjunction تا 'until'. The clause 'برآمد سپهبد ز آب' is a past event: برآمد (arose/emerged) + سپهبد (the spahbed, subject) + ز آب 'from the water' marking source. Together: 'until the spahbed emerged from the water.'
segments.s175-g101: This clause expresses motion/turning: the prepositional phrase 'ز بالا' (from above/high) + 'به پستی' (toward low/low ground) gives the path, followed by the past verb بپیچید (twisted/turned). The proper name 'گیو' follows the verb (poetic inversion) and serves as the subject performing the action.
segments.s175-g102: گریزان همی شد is a compound: گریزان (fleeing) + همی (continuous marker) + شد (became) => 'he began to flee / became fleeing'. ز marks the source/agent of flight: 'ز سالار نیو' = 'from the commander (سالار) نیو'; نیو appears as a name or epithet here (poetic/archaic), treated as the possessor/agent following سالار.
segments.s175-g103: چو introduces a temporal clause 'when'. 'از آب' = 'from the water'. وز is an archaic/poetic connective (here functioning like و + ز 'and/from') linking another source 'لشکرش' (his army). دور کرد is a causative-perfect construction 'made (someone) distant' i.e. 'drove off / put at a distance.' The actor/affected referents are supplied by context (typical in epic narration).
segments.s175-g104: The prepositional phrase 'به زین اندر' = 'into the saddle' (به = into/toward, زین = saddle, اندر = within). افگند is the past verb 'threw/cast'. The object 'گرز نبرد' is a noun compound 'mace of battle' (گرز = mace, نبرد qualifies it as 'war-/battle-'), placed after the verb in poetic order: 'he threw the war-mace into the saddle.'
segments.s175-g9: نماند is a past third-person singular form (ن + ماند) meaning 'remained not' / 'was left no more' — here it carries a negative sense 'nothing remained'. برین is the preposition بر 'on/upon' combined with the demonstrative -ین (an archaic/poetic spelling of این) yielding 'on this/that'; بوم 'homeland/soil' follows the preposition. The coordinated phrase 'و بر خاک و آب' lists two further locations ('on the earth and in the water'), with و linking the items before the verb.
segments.s175-g10: وزین here is an adjective meaning 'weighty/grave' modifying داغ ('wound' or 'burning grief'); the phrase وزین داغ دل = 'this grievous wound of the heart'. گردد (from گشت/گردیدن) is used as 'becomes' or 'came upon' (3rd sg.), and the subject افراسیاب appears postverbally (typical poetic word order), so the whole means 'a heavy grief came upon Afrasiab's heart.'
segments.s175-g11: به گفتار او is a prepositional phrase 'by/at his word' (به = 'by/in the matter of' here, indexical sense), with گفتار 'speech/word' and او 'he' as possessor. سر برافراختند is a past plural transitive verb phrase: سر ('head') placed before the verb brafraxhtand ('they raised up') — برافراختند is the past plural of برافراشتن 'to raise up'.
segments.s175-g12: شب و روز is an adverbial phrase 'night and day' placed at the front for emphasis. یکسر همی are two continuous/intensive adverbs — یکسر 'entirely/continuously' and همی (an older continuous marker) — which together qualify the verb تاختند (past plural 'they rushed/galloped/attacked'). The word order (adverbials before the verb) and use of همی give a continuous/relentless sense.
segments.s175-g13: نجستند is from جستن and here means 'sought' or 'searched for' (with a strong implication they could not obtain it) and is in the past plural. روز و شب 'day and night' is the adverbial phrase following the verb, while آرام و خواب are the direct objects ('calm and sleep') coordinated by و. The overall sense is 'they sought/failed to find rest or sleep day and night.'
segments.s175-g14: وزین آگهی is a noun phrase 'this weighty/grave news' (وزین = 'grave/weighty' modifying آگهی 'news/information'). شد به افراسیاب is a passive or result construction meaning 'was made to Afrasiab' → idiomatically 'reached Afrasiab' or 'became known to Afrasiab' (شد used as 'happened/was brought').
segments.s175-g15: چنین is an adverb 'thus/so'. تا here introduces the event that follows (classical use 'until/when'), and بیامد is the past singular 'came'. یکی is an indefinite 'one / a certain' preceding the noun (a common poetic device), and ژرف رود is 'deep river' (ژرف 'deep' + رود 'river'). Word order is somewhat inverted for poetic effect (verb before the noun phrase).
segments.s175-g16: سپه شد پراگنده is a simple predication: سپه 'the army' (subject) + شد 'became' + پراگنده 'scattered' (predicate). چون introduces a simile 'like/as', and تار و پود ('warp and weft') is the comparative image, i.e., scattered in all directions like the threads of a fabric.
segments.s175-g25: بدو گفت is an old-fashioned word order where بدو (to him) precedes the verb گفت (said): literally 'said to him'. کای is the vocative particle 'O' addressing someone; مرد is the addressee 'man'. با رنج خیز is an imperative clause: با رنج ('with suffering/hardship') modifies the imperative خیز ('rise!'), so together 'O man, rise despite hardship.'
segments.s175-g26: که introduces an explanatory subordinate clause ('for/that'). آمد is the past/simple perfect 'has come'. ت + را here form ترا ('to you' / 'upon you') as a dative/accusative clitic. روزگار گریز is a noun phrase where روزگار ('time' / 'fate') is qualified by the following noun گریز ('flight/escape'), so 'the time/fate of flight'; overall: 'for the time of your flight has come.'
segments.s175-g27: ترا‌گر is a conditional formula: ترا ('you' as object) + گر (if) = 'if they find you'. بیابند is the subjunctive/present plural form 'they should find/they find', and بیجان کنند is a causative phrase: بیجان ('lifeless') + کنند ('they make') meaning 'they will make (you) lifeless' (i.e. kill you).
segments.s175-g28: دل ما ('our heart') is the affected theme; ز (poetic from/because of) introduces the cause 'ز درد تو' ('because of your pain/for your sake' — here 'dرد' is emotional sorrow). پیچان کنند is another causative verb phrase: پیچان (twisting/writhing) + کنند ('they make') = 'they will make [our] hearts writhe.' Word order places the object (دل ما) before the cause and verb for emphasis.
segments.s175-g29: This clause is an idiomatic, somewhat inverted expression: مرا با پسر ('me with (my) son') is the incidental phrase 'on seeing me with my son' and is followed by دیده گردد پرآب where دیده ('the eye') is the subject and گردد ('becomes') + پرآب ('full of water/teary') is the predicate: 'my eye will become full of tears (at that sight)'. The object pronoun مرا is understood as what will be seen.
segments.s175-g30: برد بسته is a compact clause meaning '(they) will take (him) bound'—بسته ('bound/tied') modifies the implied direct object (مرا). تا پیش افراسیاب uses تا + پیش = 'as far as / to the presence of' (تا پیش) and names the destination: افراسیاب. In other words: 'they will bind and carry (me) to Afrasiab.'
segments.s175-g31: وزان پس ('and from that afterwards') is a transitional temporal phrase. ندانم is the first-person present negative 'I do not know' (n‑ + دانم). چه آید گزند is a subordinate interrogative/indefinite clause: چه ('what') + آید ('may come/happen') + گزند ('harm'), literally 'what harm may come.'
segments.s175-g32: نداند کسی is an impersonal/general statement where نداند ('does not know') with کسی in the sense 'no one' yields 'no one knows'. راز چرخ بلند is a noun phrase 'the secret (راز) of the lofty/high (بلند) heavens/sky (چرخ)' — a common poetic way to refer to inscrutable fate or the heavens.
segments.s175-g41: ز (preposition) + دام بلا is a prepositional phrase 'from the snare of calamity'. یافتم is a past-tense verb 'I found' and takes من (emphatic/subject) after it; رها is a predicate/adjective meaning 'free' — together یافتم من رها = 'I found myself free' (poetic word order reverses predicate and subject).
segments.s175-g42: تو is the vocative/subject 'you'. چندین here functions adverbially 'long / repeatedly / for long' (poetic use). مشو is the negative imperative/negative present of شدن 'do not remain / do not become / do not tarry'. در + دم is the locative 'in the breath' and اژدها is the noun 'dragon' — together 'do not linger in the dragon's breath.'
segments.s175-g43: به هامون is a prepositional phrase 'to the plain'. مرارفت is a poetic verbal form related to رفتن ('to go' / 'to pass'); here it expresses the action 'go/advance' (inverted word order). باید کنون places the modal 'it is necessary / one must' with کنون 'now' — together 'now one must go to the plain.'
segments.s175-g44: فشاندن is the infinitive/masdar 'to scatter / to shed' (here used as the action to be done). به شمشیر is an instrumental/agent phrase 'with / by the sword' (به used poetically as 'by/with'). بر + شید means 'upon the beloved' (بر = on/upon, شید = beloved), and خون is the object 'blood' — 'to scatter blood upon the beloved with the sword.'
segments.s175-g45: بدو is an old/poetic prepositional/clitic form meaning 'to him / unto him' placed before the verb. گفت is the past tense 'said' (here 'he said to him'); گیو‌ای is the vocative 'O Giv' (the -ای is a vocative particle attached to the name), and شه سرفراز is an appositive epithet 'victorious king' addressing him.
segments.s175-g46: جهان را uses the object marker را to mark 'the world' (here topic/what is coming). به نام تو is the idiom 'in the name of you / by your name' (به = 'in/with' + نام = 'name' + تو = 'your'). آمد نیاز is a compound: آمد (past 'came') + نیاز ('supplication/entreaty') = 'has come in supplication' or 'came to supplicate' — 'the world has come to entreat in your name.'
segments.s175-g47: پدر پهلوانست is a nominal sentence 'the father is a champion' (پهلوانست = 'is heroic / is a champion'). و connects clauses. من پهلوان is 'I am a champion' — in poetry the copula is often omitted, so 'من پهلوان' = 'I (am) a champion.'
segments.s175-g48: به شاهی is a prepositional phrase 'for / in exchange for kingship' (به indicating purpose/aim). نپیچیم is the negative present/subjunctive/optative 'we do not turn / we will not turn' (here idiomatically 'will not give/turn over'); جان و روان is a coordinated object 'life and soul' — together 'I/we will not turn (give) life and soul for kingship' i.e. 'I will not barter my life and soul for the throne.'
segments.s175-g145: Subject (سیاوش) comes first, then the prepositional phrase به گفتار او ('according to/to his speech/advice') modifies the verb. سر بداد is a poetic verb phrase: سر (literally 'head') plus بداد (past) yields the idiom 'سر دادن' meaning 'to yield/surrender' or 'to submit/obey' in epic usage; here it functions as a simple past tense clause 'he yielded'. The personal pronoun او is the object of the preposition به.
segments.s175-g146: This is a two-part conditional: گر او باد شد 'if he became wind' uses گر (if) + noun phrase او باد + شد (past 'became'). The second clause این شود نیز باد employs شود (present/subjunctive 'become') plus the final باد functioning as an optative particle ('may it be so' / 'so be it'); thus the line plays on باد as noun in the protasis and as an optative particle in the apodosis. نیز 'also' links the wished result to the earlier clause.
segments.s175-g147: ابر is the grammatical subject 'a cloud'; شاه پیران is a compressed genitive/appositive phrase ('the venerable/old king' or 'the king of the elders') attached in poetic diction to identify who is affected; گرفت is the past tense verb 'took/seized'; آفرین here is a noun 'acclaim/praise' (or acclamation) serving as the object of گرفت. The line uses elliptical, elevated word order typical of epic verse.
segments.s175-g148: خروشان is an adverbial/participle ('roaring', 'with a roar') modifying the verb that follows. ببوسید is the simple past third-person singular 'he kissed'. روی زمین is a prepositional phrase with روی used as 'upon/on' and زمین as its object; the subject is understood from the previous clause (the king).
segments.s175-g149: همی گفت is a habitual/continuous verbal phrase ('he kept saying' or 'he was saying'), where همی is an iterative/adverbial particle common in classical Persian. کای is the archaic vocative ('O'), followed by شاه (king) and دانش‌پژوه (appositive 'student/scholar' addressing the addressee). The phrase is a direct address introduced by the ongoing-report verb.
segments.s175-g150: چو is the comparative/simile particle 'like/as'. خورشید تابان is a noun + adjective sequence 'the shining sun' (adjective after noun as usual in Persian poetry). میان گروه is a prepositional phrase 'among the company/host' with میان before its noun. Together the clause supplies a simile comparing the addressee to a shining sun among a group.
segments.s175-g151: تو دانسته‌ای is a second-person perfective/present ('you know' / 'you have known') with دانسته‌ای built from the perfect participle + personal ending. درد و تیمار من is the coordinated object 'my pain and my care', where من is the possessor (postposed pronoun) as in classical Persian. The clause states that the addressee is aware of the speaker's affliction and solicitude.
segments.s175-g152: ز بهر تو is a frozen poetic prepositional phrase meaning 'for your sake' (ز = 'for/because of' + بهر = 'for, for the sake of'). با شاه پیگار من places the preposition با ('with') before شاه ('the king') and names the relation پیگار من ('my struggle/battle'); the whole clause means that on your account the speaker's fight/struggle has been with the king. Word order reflects classical compression (possessive من follows پیگار).
segments.s175-g89: هزارید is a present-tense copular/to-be form meaning ‘you are (many)’, here addressing a plural enemy (‘you number thousands’). و is the coordinating conjunction ‘and/but’ linking the contrast clause. من is the first-person subject pronoun; نامور is an adjective/postpositive epithet meaning ‘renowned’; یک دلیر is a noun phrase ‘one brave (man)’ with یک emphasizing singularity — the whole phrase is a poetic inversion stressing that a single famed warrior stands against thousands.
segments.s175-g90: سر is the direct object ‘head(s)’ (literal head). سرکشان here refers to the proud/rebellious ones (the genitive/possessive relation: ‘heads of the proud’) — note that this is a poetic pluralized modifier. اندر آرم is a classical/poetic verb phrase: آرم is an archaic first-person form (optative/indicative) meaning ‘I put/bring’ and اندر + به زیر form a directional/adverbial phrase meaning ‘into/beneath’ (به زیر = ‘under, beneath’), so the clause says ‘I will put the heads of the proud beneath.’
segments.s175-g91: چو is the subordinating conjunction ‘when/if’. من is the subject ‘I’. گرزه is the noun ‘mace’ (instrument). سرگرای here is a poetic/archaic form attached to گرزه (contextually ‘taking up/wielding the mace’), and آورم is the first-person present/subjunctive of آوردن ‘I bring/take’ — together the clause means ‘when I take up/wield the mace’.
segments.s175-g92: سران is the plural of سر in the sense ‘leaders/heads’ and is marked as definite object by را. همه (‘all’) quantifies them. زیر پای is a fixed locative phrase ‘under (the) foot’, and آورم (repeated) is ‘I bring/put’ (first-person), so the clause literally says ‘I bring all the leaders’ heads under (my) foot(s)’, i.e. ‘trample them underfoot.’
segments.s175-g93: چو introduces a temporal clause ‘when’. بشنید is the simple past ‘he/they heard’. پیران is the plural ‘elders’. برآورد here functions as a classical/periphrastic verb meaning ‘raised/produced’ (an older poetic use), and خشم is the object ‘anger’ — so the clause reads ‘when the elders heard, (they) raised anger’ or more naturally ‘when the elders heard, anger arose.’
segments.s175-g94: دلش combines دل ‘heart’ with the enclitic possessive ش ‘his’ = ‘his heart’. گشت is the past of شدن ‘became’. پرخون is a compound adjective ‘full of/bathed in blood’ (poetically indicating grief or inner bleeding). و links the two predicates, and پرآب چشم means ‘eyes full of water’ (i.e. eyes filled with tears). The clause thus states his heart turned blood-filled and his eyes were filled with tears.
segments.s175-g95: برانگیخت is the simple past ‘he roused’ or ‘he spurred/awoke’. اسپ is the object ‘steed/horse’. و is the coordinating conjunction. بیفشارد (classical past form) = ‘he pressed/pressed down’ (from فشردن) and ران is the noun ‘thigh’ (here meaning he pressed with his thigh to urge the horse on). The clause describes vigorous motion: he spurred the horse and urged it with his thigh.
segments.s175-g96: به گردن is a prepositional phrase ‘to/on (his) neck’ (به + noun). برآورد here is a poetic past tense meaning ‘he set/put (on)’ or ‘he brought/placed’. گرز گران is a noun phrase ‘heavy mace’ (گرز = mace, گران = heavy/weighty). Together: ‘he placed the heavy mace on his neck’ (i.e. slung or bore the heavy club on/over his shoulder/neck).
segments.s175-g105: گریزان is a participial adjective meaning 'fleeing' placed before the source phrase ازو ('from him'), together modifying the noun phrase پهلوان بلند ('the tall champion'). The order is emphatic/poetic: 'the champion, tall and fleeing from him.'
segments.s175-g106: ز is the poetic preposition 'from'; فتراک is 'saddle-cloth' (source). بگشاد here is a past verb form 'opened / unfolded / drew out' (ب + گشاد). پیچان is an adjective 'twisting' modifying کمند 'lasso/snare' (direct object). The phrase describes taking out/unfolding a twisting lasso from the saddle-cloth.
segments.s175-g107: هم‌آورد functions as an adjective/noun 'a match / equal (in strength)' and is followed by با گیو 'with Giv' to mean 'equal to Giv'. نزدیک شد is a compound predicate 'became near / approached' (نزدیک + شد). Word order places the qualificative (هم‌آورد) before the with-phrase and the verbal predicate.
segments.s175-g108: چون is the simile marker 'like / as'; جهان is the subject 'the world'. شب تیره تاریک is a doubled adjectival description ('like a night, dark and gloomy') used for emphasis. شد is the past verb 'became', so the clause reads 'the world became like a dark night.'
segments.s175-g109: بپیچید is a past-tense verb 'he twisted / wound' (the prefix بـ is prothetic here); the following proper name گیو is the subject. سرافراز modifies یال: 'the proud/lofty mane.' Poetic word order puts the verb before the subject and places the adjective before the noun it qualifies.
segments.s175-g110: کمند اندرافگند is an older/epic past construction meaning 'he cast/put the lasso around/into' (اندرافگند = 'caused to fall in / cast in'). و کردش is 'and he made it' where -ش is the object clitic 'it/him'; دوال is 'twofold / pair'—so 'and (he) made it twofold' (i.e., doubled or doubled-up).
segments.s175-g111: سر پهلوان is a genitive/possessive group 'the head of the champion'. اندر آمد is the past verbal phrase 'entered / came into' and به بند uses به + بند to indicate entering 'into the bond/snare' (بند = fetter/snare). The clause reports that the champion's head entered the noose.
segments.s175-g112: ز marks source 'from the saddle' (زین). برگرفتش is the past verb برگرفت 'took up / seized' with the object clitic -ش 'him/it' attached, so 'he took him from the saddle.' به خم کمند 'into the bend/curve of the lasso' uses به to mark direction/goal (into). Together: he lifted/took him from the saddle into the lasso's loop.
segments.s175-g49: This archaic phrase uses مرا هست (literally “there is to me”) to express possession: “I have …”. Word order is topical: برادر (brother) is fronted, مرا هست supplies the existential/possessive sense, and هفتاد و هشت is the numeral that specifies how many. Note that مرا is split in the word list into parts, but functions here as the dative/possessive element.
segments.s175-g50: جهان شد is a simple verbal clause: جهان (the world/the realm) + شد (became/was transformed). چو introduces a temporal clause “when,” and نام تو اندر گذشت is an intransitive clause “your name passed in/through/was spoken.” The order is typical classical Persian: main verb (شد) before the subordinate time clause introduced by چو.
segments.s175-g51: بسی here functions adverbially to mean “many/much” (classical usage), followed by پهلوانست, where the copula -ست attaches to the predicate to say “there are heroes.” شاه اندکی reverses expected order for emphasis: شاه (kings) + اندکی (are few). The construction contrasts a plentiful class (heroes) with a scarce one (kings).
segments.s175-g52: This is a rhetorical question. چه باشد is an exclamatory/idle interrogative (“what is/worth”), with باشد in the optative/indicative sense. چو introduces the subordinate clause “when/if,” and پیدا نباشد یکی means “if even one is not found.” نباشد is the negative present/subjunctive form (“is not / be not”), and یکی emphasizes a single exemplar.
segments.s175-g53: اگر … شوم is a conditional clause: اگر + personal pronoun + شوم (1st‑person subjunctive of شدن) gives “if I become …/if I am …”. کشته is a past participle meaning “slain/killed.” دیگر بود follows as the apodosis: “there would be another.” The sequence expresses a hypothetical replacement after death.
segments.s175-g54: This short clause names a consequence/result. سر here functions as “head/chief,” followed by تاجور (a noun in apposition or genitive relation), then باشد (subjunctive/optative “may be/is”) and افسر بود (literally “was a helmet/officer”); in poetic diction nouns and predicates can be juxtaposed elliptically. The line implies a change of status — grammatically a nominal clause with a copula chain.
segments.s175-g55: Conditional protasis: اگر + تو + شوی (2nd‑person subjunctive/present of شدن) = “if you become.” دور از ایدر is a prepositional phrase “distant from here,” and تباه is a predicative adjective “ruined/corrupt.” Word order places the condition first, then the state/result.
segments.s175-g56: نبینم is the 1st‑person negative present/subjunctive of دیدن (“I do not see / I shall not see”). کسی is the indefinite object “anyone/no one.” از در is a prepositional phrase where در means “door/gate,” and تاج و گاه (‘crown and time’) is a compound genitive phrase denoting the realm/occasion of rulership; the phrase as a whole expresses the speaker’s inability to find anyone at those gates. Word order is verb + object + locative phrase.
segments.s175-g73: اگر introduces a conditional protasis (‘if’). کوه آهن بود is a nominal phrase with بود (past/be) giving a counterfactual sense ‘were (made of) iron’. یک سوار follows as the subject/agent of the main clause that the condition sets up.
segments.s175-g74: چو introduces a circumstantial/comparative clause here ‘as/when’. مور اندر آید literally ‘enters within like an ant’ (آید = comes/enters). به گردش هزار is a prepositional phrase ‘into/for a thousand circuits’ indicating the number of turns — گردش = circuit/turn, with هزار (thousand) quantifying it.
segments.s175-g75: شود is an impersonal/third‑person present/future‑oriented form ‘becomes’ (literary usage). خیره‌سر is a predicate adjective ‘headstrong’; گرچه introduces the concessive clause ‘although’, followed by خردست ‘is small’ modifying مور (the ant).
segments.s175-g76: نه مورست is a negative statement: نه + (مور است contracted) = ‘it is not an ant / not only an ant’. پوشیده here is predicative ‘hidden/concealed’, and مرد و ستور names the concealed things (‘man and steed’). Word order is tersely poetic: the copula is folded into مورست and the items concealed follow.
segments.s175-g77: کنند is third‑person plural (literary narrative) ‘they make/do’ or simply the plural verb ‘they [do …]’. این زره بر تنش is the object phrase ‘this armor on his body’ (ش = his). چاک چاک is a reduplicated adjective ‘gashed, all split’, following the object as a vivid predicate.
segments.s175-g78: چو here = ‘when’. مردار گردد uses گردد (گردیدن/گشتن ‘become’) with مردار ‘carrion’ to mean ‘when he becomes carrion’. کشندش is a compound form (کشند + ـش object suffix) meaning ‘they drag/haul him’ (not ‘kill’ in this context), and به خاک is the idiom ‘into the dust/ground’.
segments.s175-g79: یکی داستان زد is a compact narrative formula: یکی = ‘a certain/one (tale)’, داستان = ‘story’, زد (from زدن) here = ‘relate/utter’ (literary use). هژبر دمان is a proper name or epithet in apposition to the narrator.
segments.s175-g80: که introduces the content clause ‘that…’. چون here functions temporally ‘when/whenever’, بر گوزنی = ‘upon a deer/stag’ (بر + accusative noun), and سرآید زمان is a fixed phrasing ‘the time comes’ (سرآید = arrive/happen, زمان = time/moment) — together: ‘that when the time comes upon a stag…’.
segments.s175-g169: This clause uses the idiom سوگند خوردن (to take an oath) with خوردن as the verb; یکی سخت qualifies the oath as 'one (very) severe/solemn' and precedes سوگند, while به introduces the standard oath-grounds (here به ماه). Word order is typical Persian: determiner/adverb (یکی سخت) + noun (سوگند) + verb (خوردم) + prepositional phrase (به ماه).
segments.s175-g170: A continuation of the list of things sworn by: repeated preposition به (instrumental 'by') governs تاج and then تخت. شه نیک‌خواه is a noun phrase (شه = king) followed by the epithet نیک‌خواه 'benevolent/well‑wishing'; word order is preposition + head noun, with the epithet after the head.
segments.s175-g171: This is a conditional protasis headed by که‌گر (archaic/poetic 'if'). دست یابم is the verb phrase 'I attain/get hold (of)' where یابم is present-subjunctive/optative first person singular ('may I attain' / 'I can attain'). برو روز is an archaic poetic locative—here meaning 'on the day' (بِر / بر + روز), followed by the noun کین 'vengeance'; the clause thus sets the condition and the temporal frame.
segments.s175-g172: Main clause: کنم is the present/future‑oriented first person singular of کردن 'I will do/make', taking a result phrase ارغوانی ز خونش زمین 'make the earth purple from his blood'. ز marks source/material ('from/with'), and the adjective ارغوانی modifies the resulting state of زمین. Word order: verb (کنم) + complement (color + source + object).
segments.s175-g173: This is a speech-reporting phrase: بدو 'to him' (prepositional dative) + گفت (reported-speech verb). کیخسرو‌ای is the vocative 'O Kay Khosrow' addressing the speaker, and شیرفش is a proper name/apostrophe. Word order places the indirect object/recipient (بدو) before the verb and then the vocative.
segments.s175-g174: An imperative prohibition: زبان را (direct object marked by را) refers to 'speech/words' (زبان used as 'utterance'). ز سوگند یزدان is an ablative/instrumental phrase 'by the oath of God' (ز = from/by), and مکش is the negative imperative 'do not draw/withdraw' (here idiomatically 'do not retract your words / do not break your oath').
segments.s175-g175: Imperative instruction: کنونش 'now' (temporal adverb) precedes the causative-imperative construction به سوگند گستاخ کن — 'make him/give him audacity/impudence by means of an oath'. گستاخ is an object/complement meaning 'insolent/audacious', and کن is the imperative 'do/make'.
segments.s175-g176: A second imperative: a prepositional instrument به + خنجر 'with a dagger', then the locative/target وراگوش 'behind the ear', followed by سوراخ‌کن (imperative or agentive participle) meaning 'pierce/make a hole'. The sequence gives manner/instrument + target + action.
segments.s175-g193: بدان گشت is a verbal phrase where ب- (a prefixed be-) attaches to دان and is followed by گشت 'became/turned', producing the sense 'became of that mind / agreed to that'. همداستان پهلوان is the subject phrase 'the champion, like‑minded'; in classical Persian the predicate (گشت) can precede the subject, so the whole means 'the champion became like-minded about that.'
segments.s175-g194: به سوگند is an adverbial prepositional phrase 'by/with an oath' (به used in the sense 'by'). بخرید is a past narration form 'bought' (classical past) or a narrative past here 'he bought'; اسپ و روان are the direct objects joined by و. The literal sequence places the adverbial before the verb and objects after it.
segments.s175-g195: که introduces a relative/purpose clause 'that/so that'. نگشاید is a negative form of the verb 'to open' (classical form expressing 'would not / did not open'); آن بند را is the definite object marked by را ('that fetter/the bond'), and کس is the (indefinite) subject 'anyone' placed after the object (OSV order). به راه is a locative phrase 'on the road'.
segments.s175-g196: ز گلشهر سازد وی is a clause with the preposition ز 'from' plus the place/name گلشهر, followed by سازد 'he made/fashioned' and the subject pronoun وی 'he' (postposed for emphasis). آن دستگاه is the definite object 'that device/arrangement' receiving the action. The verb سازد is present/habitual or narrative past in classical style meaning 'he made'.
segments.s175-g197: بدو داد اسپ is a simple transitive clause: بدو 'to him' (indirect object), داد 'gave' (past), اسپ 'a horse' (direct object). و دو دستش ببست follows: و 'and' links another clause/object, دو دستش 'his two hands' (possessive clitic ش on دست), and ببست is the past 'he tied/bound' — here the object precedes the verb (common classical order).
segments.s175-g198: ازان پس 'after that/thereupon' is a temporal connector. بفرمود is an honorific past 'he commanded/said' (used for a ruler's speech or order). تا here introduces a purpose/result clause 'that/so that' and برنشست is the verb 'he sat upon / mounted' (perfective past form used in narrative).
segments.s175-g161: چنان دان که is an imperative construction: چنان (ʼthus/soʼ) + دان (imperative/consider ‘know/consider’) introduces a subordinate clause with که (‘that’). The demonstrative این ('this') directly precedes پیرسر پهلوان (‘gray‑headed champion’), so word order is: imperative → complementizer → demonstrative + noun phrase.
segments.s175-g162: A string of predicates/adjectives linked by و: خردمند و رادست و روشن‌روان modifies the preceding noun پهلوان. There is no repeated copula; these adjectives form a coordinated attributive phrase (poetic compounds like روشن‌روان = ‘bright‑souled’). رادست is an archaic adjective meaning generous/noble here.
segments.s175-g163: پس از here functions as a prepositional/temporal phrase (‘so/after/from’) introducing the source of guidance: داور دادگر رهنمون = ‘the just judge (as) guide’. The noun phrase that follows (داور دادگر) is the head; رهنمون (‘guide’) names the role.
segments.s175-g164: بدان is the prefixed form ب + دان (ب acting as the inseparable prefix here, + دان = ‘know/consider’), so بداندان → ‘know/consider that…’. کاو in classical Persian functions as a relative pronoun ‘who/which’ introducing the relative clause; here it heads کاو رهانید ما را ز خون (‘who freed us from blood’). رهانید is past tense ‘freed/released’ (third person), ما را is the object with را, and ز خون = ‘from blood’ (i.e. from bloodshed). Note: the supplied senses list for کاو is not the usual relative‑pronoun sense; in this line کاو acts as ‘who’.
segments.s175-g165: ز بد مهر او is a fronted prepositional phrase: ز = ‘because of/from’, بد مهر = ‘ill‑will/bad affection’, او = ‘his’. پرده جان ماست = ‘a veil of our life is’ with پرده جان (‘veil of the life/soul’) followed by the copula ست (‘is’) at the end — typical Persian noun + copula order.
segments.s175-g166: وزین here is best read as وزین = و + زین in archaic/poetic spelling or as the adjective ‘dignified/weighty’; syntactically it begins a participial clause: وزین کرده خویش = ‘having made himself grave/dignified’ (کرده = perfective ‘made/doing’, خویش = reflexive ‘his own’). زنهار خواست = ‘he asked for zenehār’ — zنهار can mean ‘warning/admonition’ or an entreaty to refrain; خواست is past tense ‘he sought/asked’.
segments.s175-g167: بدو گفت = ‘he said to him’ (بدو = ‘to him/unto him’, گفت = past ‘said’). گیو‌ای is a vocative form (گیو + ای) addressing Giv, and سر بانوان = ‘chief/head of the ladies’ is a vocative title appended after the name. Word order: indirect object + verb + vocative phrase.
segments.s175-g168: انوشه روان is a poetic personal name/epithet (Anoushe‑ruvan = ‘Immortal‑soul’ or ‘Anoushe, the soul’). باش is the imperative ‘be’ (wish/command), and تا جاودان uses تا = ‘until/so that’ with جاودان ‘eternal’ to express the desired duration: ‘be (so) until eternity’ → ‘be eternal/forever’. This is a conventional benediction phrasing.
segments.s175-g113: This clause describes the manner and action: پیاده (an adverbial/nominal adverb 'on foot') sets the scene; به پیش ('towards/in front') gives directional/manner nuance; اندر افگند is the past verb 'afgand' (threw/cast into) with اندر reinforcing motion/placement; خوار is a predicative/adverbial describing the resulting state ('humiliated/low'). The verb is in simple past (poetic diction) and the object is implicit (the person thrown).
segments.s175-g114: ببردش is a narrative past form meaning 'he took/led him' with the enclitic -ش = 'him/it' as object; دمان ('daman') means the hem/skirt and is the part by which he grabs him; تا + لب + رودبار is a prepositional phrase marking extent ('as far as the brink of the river-valley').
segments.s175-g115: بیفگند is a past tense verb 'he threw/ cast (down)'; بر خاک is the prepositional phrase 'on the ground'; و links clauses; دستش combines دست 'hand' + enclitic ش 'his/him' to mark whose hands, and ببست is the past verb 'he bound/tied' (here 'bound his hands').
segments.s175-g116: سلیحش = سلیح (weapon) + enclitic -ش 'his' is the direct object; بپوشید is the past/simple perfect 'he put on / donned' (here, his arms); و connects the next action; خود (reflexive/emphatic 'himself') + بر + نشست 'sat down' — نشست is a past-tense verb 'sat/settled'.
segments.s175-g117: درفشش is درفش (banner) + enclitic -ش 'his' (his banner); گرفته is a participial/past adjective/verb 'taken/seized' describing that he has the banner; به چنگ is an idiom meaning 'into the grasp' (به = towards/into, چنگ = grip/hand figuratively); اندرون emphasizes movement inward/within. The phrase as a whole means 'having seized his banner in his hand'.
segments.s175-g118: بشد is a simple past/preterite form meaning 'he went/moved / it came to pass' in narrative; تا + لب + آب + گلزریون marks the extent/destination 'until the bank of the Gulzariun water' (تا = until, لب = brink/bank, آب = water, گلزریون is a place/name).
segments.s175-g119: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'; ترکان is the subject 'the Turks'; درفش سپهدار خویش is a genitive phrase 'the banner of their sepahdar/commander' (درفش 'banner', سپهدار 'commander', خوی + ش together form 'خویش' = 'their own'). Word order is typical Persian: temporal marker + subject + object phrase.
segments.s175-g120: بدیدند is the past-plural 'they saw'; رفتند is the past-plural 'they went/advanced'; ناچار functions adverbially/adjectivally 'compelled/inevitably' modifying رفتند; پیش here is an adverb meaning 'forward / to the front' (movement toward the front). Together: 'they saw, and necessarily moved forward.'
segments.s175-g153: سزدگر is an archaic/poetic conditional particle meaning “if it be fitting” (literally “one who deems fit”); من is the first‑person subject. از + چنگ این اژدها is a prepositional phrase “from/out of the claw of this dragon” (چنگ = claw/talon). Word order is classical/poetic: the conditional particle + subject + prepositional phrase before the predicate that follows.
segments.s175-g154: به بخت و به فر تو repeats the preposition به for emphasis and lists two means: بخت (“fortune/luck”) and فر (“farr,” i.e. royal/divine splendour) belonging to تو (you). یابم is a 1st‑person subjunctive/imprecative form “may I find/attain,” and رها is predicative “free/unbound” — together یابم رها = “may I find myself freed.” This clause is the apodosis to the conditional سزدگر above.
segments.s175-g155: به کیخسرو indicates the goal/target of the gaze (“toward Kay Khosrow”); اندر نگه کرد is the verb phrase “directed/turned (his) gaze” (نگه = gaze, کرد = did/caused); گیو appears after the verb as the subject in this poetic word order (subject after verb is common in classical Persian).
segments.s175-g156: بدان is the fusion of the prefix بِ- + دان (“to know” = ‘in order to know’); تا introduces the content/purpose (“so that / in order that”) and governs the indirect question چه فرمان دهد — چه = “what,” فرمان = “command,” دهد is a present/subjunctive form used for reported/future sense “(will) give.” شاه نیو (شاه + نیو ‘new’) is the subject of the subordinate clause placed after the verb in poetic order.
segments.s175-g157: فرنگیس را دید is a straightforward object construction with را marking the definite direct object (“saw Farangis”). دیده پرآب is a nominative/absolutely attributive phrase meaning “(her) eye/eyes full of water” (پرآب = literally ‘full of water’ → ‘teary’); it follows the object to describe her condition.
segments.s175-g158: زبان پر ز نفرین affirms that her speech/tongue is “full of curse(s)” — زبان here = ‘speech/utterance’ rather than merely the organ; پر = ‘full (of)’; ز is the poetic genitive/linking preposition ‘of/from’; نفرین = ‘curse.’ افراسیاب after نفرین names the source/target: either ‘the curse of Afrasiab’ or ‘cursing Afrasiab’ depending on context, a normal poetic genitive ordering.
segments.s175-g159: به گیو is the indirect object “to Giv”; آن زمان is a temporal adverbial “at that time/then”; گفت is the past verb “said” (here introducing direct speech); کای is the vocative particle “O,” and سرافراز is the vocative epithet “exalted/honoured one.” Word order places the recipient and time before the verb, and the vocative opens the reported speech.
segments.s175-g160: کشیدی is a past 2nd‑person perfect/preterite “you endured/you suffered” (the addressee is Giv). بسی Ranj = “much hardship” (بسی = very/much; رنج = suffering). راه دراز is a noun phrase “the long road” specifying where/over which the suffering was borne; Persian often states the object (بسی رنج) then adds the circumstantial specification (راه دراز).
segments.s175-g129: به شمشیر و با نیزه is a means phrase: two parallel prepositional phrases 'به شمشیر' and 'با نیزه' (‘by/with the sword and with the spear’) that indicate the instruments used. سرگرای is an adjectival/participial form meaning ‘being occupied’ or ‘engaged’ and here functions adverbially to modify the following verb (it explains how he acted). The order (instrument phrases before the participle) is typical of classical/poetic Persian.
segments.s175-g130: همی is the old progressive particle giving continuous aspect (‘kept/was’). کشت is the past (poetic) form of kandan/kaštan ‘kill’ here meaning ‘was killing/kept slaying.’ ازیشان (از + ایشان) is the object phrase ‘of them / them’ and marks who is being killed. یل رهنمای is the postposed subject (noun phrase 'the hero, the guide') — in epic poetry the subject often comes after the verb for emphasis.
segments.s175-g131: افگنده شد is a passive construction (past participle + شد) meaning ‘was cast/was thrown up’ or ‘rose.’ روی هامون (the face/surface of the plain) is the affected noun and comes after the verb (poetic inversion). چون + کوه is a simile 'like a mountain.' از here marks the idea of emergence/source (‘was thrown up from/rose’).
segments.s175-g132: ز یک تن (literally 'from/into one body') is an idiomatic expression meaning 'into one person/body' or 'as one.' شدند is the plural past 'they became.' آن دلیران is the postposed subject 'those brave ones,' and ستوه is a post‑nominal adjective meaning 'weary' describing them. The word order (verb before subject) is characteristic of epic style.
segments.s175-g133: قفای یلان is a construct phrase 'the rear/behind of the heroes' (قفا = rear). سوی او شد همه literally 'all became toward him' i.e. 'all turned toward him.' شد is the past auxiliary/verb 'became' used here to indicate the change of direction; همه ('all') follows the verb for emphasis, a frequent poetic placement.
segments.s175-g134: چو شیر is a simile 'like a lion.' اندر آمد is the verb phrase 'entered/ came in' (اندر = 'in/into' + آمد = 'came'). به پیش رمه means 'into the presence/forepart of the throng' (به پیش = toward/in front of; رمه = throng/multitude). The subject 'he' is implied and placed logically before/after the verb in the verse.
segments.s175-g135: چو here is the subordinating 'when.' لشکر هزیمت شد is a passive expression 'the army was routed/put to flight' (هزیمت = rout; شد = became/was). از پیش + name (گیو) means 'from before Giv' or 'in front of Giv' — i.e. they fled/ were routed in the presence of Giv. The temporal clause sets the scene for the following description.
segments.s175-g136: چنان لشکری is 'such a host/army.' گشن is an adjective 'hungry' describing the host. و links the adjectives/phrases; مردان is 'men' and نیو is a regional/poetic form of نو 'new' (i.e. newly‑raised or young men) modifying مردان. The phrase lists qualities of the routed troops: they were hungry and composed of new/young men.
segments.s175-g33: بدو گفت is a common classical pattern meaning “said to him/her”: بدو is the clitic prepositional pronoun ‘to him/her’ placed before the verb, and گفت is the past tense verb ‘said’. گیو‌ای is the subject with the vocative marker -ای attached (’O Giv’), and مه بانوان is an epithet in apposition functioning as a vocative address — مه = ‘great’ before بانوان ‘ladies’ (i.e. ‘O great lady’).
segments.s175-g34: چرا is the interrogative ‘why’. رنجه کردی is a past, 2nd-person singular form meaning ‘you caused yourself distress / you suffered’ (رنجه = be distressed; کردی = you did), so together ‘why have you been distressed’. بدینسان is an adverb ‘in this way/thus’. روان here qualifies the subject (you) and means ‘moving/away/on the move’ (i.e. ‘having set out’ or ‘in this state of motion’).
segments.s175-g35: تو is the emphatic/explicit 2nd‑person ‘you’. با شاه indicates accompaniment ‘with the king’. برشو is an imperative/hortative-like form ‘get up / mount / rise’ addressed to the addressee. به بالای تند is a direction/goal: به + بالای = ‘onto / to the top of’, and تند ‘swift’ likely qualifies a horse (‘the swift one’) — so ‘mount with the king onto the swift (steed)’.
segments.s175-g36: ز introduces cause/source ‘because of / on account of’. پیران و لشکر ‘the elders and the army/host’ are the things that might cause fear. مشو is the negative imperative ‘do not become / do not be’. هیچ کند is a subordinate clause with هیچ ‘anything/at all’ and کند (3rd person subjunctive/present) ‘do/make’, here expressing ‘that they do anything’ — thus the whole clause means roughly ‘do not be (alarmed) because of the elders and the army, let them do nothing.’
segments.s175-g37: This is a nominal sentence: جهاندار پیروز ‘the victorious sovereign’ (جهاندار = ruler/sovereign; پیروز = victorious) is the topic, یار = ‘companion/ally’, and منست is an archaic copular/possessive-like form (classical contraction of من است) meaning ‘is mine / is with me’. The whole line asserts that the conqueror-king is the speaker’s ally.
segments.s175-g38: سر اختر is a genitive-like phrase ‘the head/chief of the stars’ (سر here = chief/top), اندر کنار = a locative phrase ‘at/by the side of’, and منست again is the archaic copula ‘is with me / stands by me’. Grammatically this repeats the assertion in celestial imagery: the chief star is at the speaker’s side.
segments.s175-g39: بدوگفت is a fused classical phrase ‘he said to him / addressed him’ (بدو = to him, گفت = said). کیخسرو‌ای is a vocative ‘O Kay Khosrow’ (the -ای marks direct address). رزمساز is an epithet in apposition, literally ‘maker/placer of battle’ (i.e. a war‑maker or commander).
segments.s175-g40: کنون = ‘now’. بر تو is a prepositional phrase ‘upon you / concerning you’. بر کار من شد دراز is an idiomatic classical construction: بر کار من = ‘on my affair / in my business’, شد = past of شدن ‘became’, and دراز = ‘long / protracted’. Together the clause says that the speaker’s business/affair regarding the addressee has now become lengthy/protracted.
segments.s175-g17: This is a nominal (copular) clause with the past copula 'بود' at the end. 'بنش' is the subject ('seat' / pit / emplacement). 'ژرف' and the coordinated phrase 'پهناش کوتاه' are adjectival predicates: 'ژرف' modifies 'بنش' ('deep'), and 'پهناش' = 'پهنا' + pronominal suffix '-ش' ('its breadth') is linked by 'و' and described as 'کوتاه' ('short' / 'narrow'). The final 'بود' is the past tense copula ('was').
segments.s175-g18: 'بدو' is a locative/directional prepositional phrase ('to/for it' = toward that place). 'بر' here marks direction/toward (literally 'toward/on to'), followed by the infinitive 'رفتن' ('to go') used to express attempts of approach. 'دژآگاه' is a compound noun ('diz-âgâh') meaning a fortress-warden/watchful sentinel, and 'بود' is the past copula. Together the phrase says that, toward that place, a vigilant fortress-guard was stationed to oppose anyone trying to go in.
segments.s175-g19: 'نشسته' is a participial/adjectival form ('seated') used predicatively; the proper name 'فرنگیس' is the patient/subject. The prepositional phrase 'بر پاس گاه' ('on the pass-/guard-post') follows: 'بر' = 'on/upon' and 'پاسگاه' is a compound of 'پاس' (sentry) + 'گاه' (place), meaning 'sentry-post'. The copula ('was') is omitted, a normal omission in Persian: 'Farangis (was) seated on the post.'
segments.s175-g20: 'به دیگر کران' is a locative phrase ('on/at the other shore/bank'). 'خفته' is the past participle 'asleep' describing the state of the next noun. The short word 'بد' here functions in an older/poetic usage as a vestigial/copular element (compare 'بود') linking to the predicate; the clause ends with the coordinated subject 'گیو و شاه' ('Giv and the king'), so the whole means 'on the other bank Giv and the king lay asleep.'
segments.s175-g21: 'فرنگیس' is the subject. 'زان' = 'از + آن' ('from that' / 'therefrom') is a locative/prepositional particle, and 'جایگه' ('place' / position) names the vantage point. The verb 'بنگرید' is an older/epic past form of 'نگریستن' ('looked' / 'gazed') — here 3rd person past — so the segment literally reads 'Farangis looked from that place.'
segments.s175-g22: This is a compact nominal + verb clause: 'درفش سپهدار توران' is a genitive sequence 'the banner (درفش) of the Turanian spahdār/commander' (noun followed by its possessor). 'بدید' is the past tense 'saw' (epic form with the b- prefix + دید). Together: 'she saw the Turanian commander's banner.'
segments.s175-g23: 'دوان شد' is a common periphrastic past in epic Persian: literal 'became running' = 'ran' (so 'شد' functions as the past auxiliary after the verbal stem). 'بر گیو' is the directional prepositional phrase 'toward Giv.' The coordinating 'و' links the next action 'آگاه کرد': 'آگاه' ('aware') with the light verb 'کرد' ('made/did') yields 'made aware' = 'warned/alerted.' So the whole segment means 'she ran to Giv and warned him.'
segments.s175-g24: 'بران' ('on/thereupon' / 'thereon') links this action to the previous. 'خفتگان' is the plural nominal 'the sleeping ones' (the sleepers) and is the object. 'خواب کوتاه کرد' is a verb phrase where 'خواب' ('sleep') is qualified by 'کوتاه' ('short') and completed by the light verb 'کرد' ('made/did') — literally 'made the sleep short' = 'cut short their sleep' (i.e., roused them). This is a causative construction: she caused their sleep to be shortened.
segments.s175-g57: شود is an optative/volitive form meaning 'may it become' (classical Persian use of شود to express a wish). رنجِ من هفت‌ساله is a genitive phrase 'my seven‑year suffering' (هفت‌ساله = seven‑year/lasting seven years). به باد is an idiom (به باد شدن) meaning 'to be blown away/wasted' where به is the preposition and باد is literally 'wind' but functions idiomatically.
segments.s175-g58: دگر here functions as 'otherwise'/'else' (classical usage of دگر = 'other/otherwise'). آنک introduces the content clause ('that/which'), linking to what follows. ننگ آورم is 'I bring/cause shame'—آورم is 1st‑person present (or present‑subjunctive used for future/intent) meaning 'I cause/bring', and بر نژاد is the prepositional phrase 'upon my lineage' with بر = 'upon' and نژاد = 'lineage/stock'.
segments.s175-g59: تو is the 2nd‑person pronoun 'you'. بالا گزین is an imperative or hortative 'choose/take the high place' (بالا = 'up/high', گزین used as an imperative 'choose/take'). و سپه را ببین is coordinate: و = 'and', سپه را = definite object 'the army' (را marks the object), and ببین is 2nd‑person imperative 'look/observe'.
segments.s175-g60: مرا یاد باشد is an optative clause 'may there be remembrance of me' (مرا = 'me' as object; here split in the word list as م + را but grammatically مرا). یاد داشتن = 'to remember' and باشد is subjunctive/optative 'may it be'. جهان‌آفرین is the agent ('the Creator') and functions as the implied subject: 'May the Creator remember me.'
segments.s175-g61: بپوشید is a past tense (perfective) 3rd‑person form 'he put on' (بـ‑ prefix appears in classical narrative forms). درع is the direct object 'armor'. و بیامد coordinates with 'and he came'—بیامد is past 'he came'. چو شیر is a simile 'like a lion' (چو = 'as/like', شیر = 'lion').
segments.s175-g62: همان باره = 'on that same occasion / at that very moment' (همان = 'the same/that', باره = 'occasion/instance'). دستکش را is the definite object 'the glove' (را marks it). به زیر is an adverbial phrase 'down / beneath' (here meaning 'lowered the gauntlet'—the verb is implied or follows in the poem), with به indicating direction/toward and زیر = 'below'.
segments.s175-g63: ازین سوی شه بود is a classical locative inversion: ازین سوی = 'from this side' (از = 'from', این orthographically given as ین = 'this', سوی = 'side/direction'), شه بود = 'stood/was the king' (بود = past copula). ز آنسو سپاه = 'from that side (was) the army' (ز = poetic 'from', آنسو = 'that side', سپاه = 'army'); the copula is shared across the two halves.
segments.s175-g64: میانچی شده رود is a compact narrative clause: میانچی شده uses شده as a passive/perfective participle 'a messenger had been made/dispatched', and رود here functions as a verb 'go/has gone' in classical usage (the word list lacked the verbal sense for رود, so the element is verbal). و بر بسته راه = 'and the way/road was closed/blocked' (بر + بسته = 'shut/barricaded upon', راه = 'road/path'); the phrase describes that a messenger was sent and the road was shut off.
segments.s175-g177: چو is the subordinating conjunction 'when'. از marks the source ('from/out of') and combines with خنجرت (your dagger; the -ت is the 2nd-person possessive enclitic) as the source of the blood. چکد is a 3rd-person singular present/past-narrative form 'drips/trickles' used here in a temporal clause. بر زمین is a prepositional phrase 'onto the ground' placed after the verb, the usual word order in older/poetic Persian (subordinator + source + verb + locative).
segments.s175-g178: The pair هم ... هم is a correlative 'both ... and ...'. از/ز both mean 'from' (poetic z is just a variant). یاد آیدت is a composed verb: یاد 'remembrance' (noun) + آیدت ('comes to you' = آید + enclitic ت for 'to you'), so the whole clause means 'a remembrance will come to you'. The two prepositional phrases (هم از مهر ... هم ز کین) specify the two sources of remembrance: love (مهر) and hatred/vengeance (کین).
segments.s175-g179: بشد (ب + شد) is a past/narrative verb 'became/rose' used here as 'rose/stood up' with the subject name following it (poetic inversion: verb before subject). گوشش is 'his ear' (گوش + enclitic ش = his) and به خنجر بسفت is a verbal clause 'fastened/pressed to the dagger' where بسفت is a past form meaning 'he fastened/pressed'. The coordinate و links the two actions of Giv.
segments.s175-g180: ز سوگند functions as an ablative/causal phrase 'because of the oath'. برتر is comparative/adverbial 'beyond/greater than' and modifies درشتی 'rudeness, harshness'. نگفت is ن‑ + گفت (negative past) meaning 'did not say/utter'; syntactically the phrase says he did not utter (any) rudeness greater than the oath. (Note: نگفت is the negated past of گفت.)
segments.s175-g181: چنین گفت is the idiom 'thus said'; in epic word order the verb can precede the subject (گفت پیران = 'the elders said'). ازان پس is an adverbial phrase 'from that on / thereafter'. به شاه marks the indirect object 'to the king' (the addressee of their speech). گفت here is the past narrative verb 'said'.
segments.s175-g182: که introduces the reported subordinate clause 'that...'. کلباد شد is past narration 'Kalbad came / became' (شد used intransitively to report arrival/occurrence). بی‌گمان is an adverb 'undoubtedly' modifying شد. با سپاه is a prepositional phrase 'with an army' specifying accompaniment.
segments.s175-g183: بفرمای here functions as an imperative/optative 'let him order / command' (a polite/3rd-person-form command). کاسپم carries a possessive -م 'my kasp' (the exact referent is context-dependent). دهد is a subjunctive/present form 'that he give' (the subordinate verb after a command); باز 'back/again' modifies دهد to mean 'give back/return', and نیز 'also' links this instruction to whatever else was said. The clause is an order: 'Order that my X be returned as well.'
segments.s175-g184: چنان دان is an idiom 'consider/know it thus' (imperative or hortative instruction to accept the following). که introduces the content clause. بخشیده‌ای is 2nd-person perfect 'you have forgiven/you have given' (here best read 'you have spared' or 'you have pardoned'), and جان و چیز means 'life and things' = 'life and possessions'. The whole means 'consider it so: that you have spared life and property.'
segments.s175-g185: بدو (be-do) is the prepositional pronoun 'to him' placed before the verb; گفت is the simple past 3rd-singular 'said' so the phrase literally reads 'to him said'. گیو‌ای is the vocative (name + -ای) addressing Giv; دلیر سپاه is an epithet in the noun phrase, literally 'brave of the host' (attribute + head noun).
segments.s175-g186: چرا is the interrogative 'why' beginning the question. سست is a predicative adjective 'weak/faint' and گشتی here is the resultative/ perfective sense of گشتن used with an adjectival predicate to mean 'you have become' (2nd person). به + آوردگاه marks location ('in/at the battlefield'); به is functioning as 'into/at'.
segments.s175-g187: به سوگند is an instrumental/prepositional phrase 'by (an) oath' (به = 'by'). یابی is 2nd-person subjunctive/optative form of یابیدن used in a conditional sense 'you may find/recover'. مگر here functions as 'unless/if not'. باره باز combines باره 'load/spoil' with باز 'again'.
segments.s175-g188: دو دستت is a noun phrase 'your two hands' (دو = numeral + دست 'hand' + ت enclitic = 'your'). ببندم is 1st-person singular present/subjunctive form used to indicate intent/future ('I will bind' / 'let me bind'). به بند دراز is an instrumental phrase 'with a long fetter/chain' (به introducing the instrument, بند = fetter, دراز = long).
segments.s175-g189: که introduces a purpose/relative clause 'so that/that'. نگشاید is a negative jussive/subjunctive form (ن‑ + گشاید) meaning 'may not open' or 'should not be opened'. این بند تو = 'this bond of yours' (این 'this' + بند 'fetter' + تو 'your'). هیچکس is the indefinite negative 'no one', placed after the noun phrase for emphasis.
segments.s175-g190: گشاینده is an agentive participle/nominal 'opener' (one who opens). گلشهر is a proper name (Golshahr). خواهیم is 1st-person plural (here future/assertive 'we will be' / 'we shall'), and و بس is the final particle 'and that's all' / 'only'. Together they assert exclusivity: 'we alone shall be the openers of Golshahr.'
segments.s175-g191: کجا introduces a rhetorical interrogative 'where / is there anywhere'. مهتر is a comparative/title 'greater / more distinguished'. بانوان تو is the genitival phrase 'your ladies/women'. اوست is the copula 'she is' (او + ست), so the clause asks rhetorically who among your women is superior to her.
segments.s175-g192: وزو is a contracted classical sequence (و ز او = 'and from her'); نیست پیدا ترا is a negated predication 'is not found/visible to you' where پیدا functions as 'visible/apparent' and ترا (ت + را) means 'to you' (enclitic + object marker). مغز و پوست 'brain and skin' lists the things not visible — here an idiomatic complaint about lack of substance or inner worth. The whole segment says that from her nothing of 'brain' (sense of intellect/substance) or 'flesh' is apparent to you.
segments.s175-g137: چنان is an adverb ('so/thus') that modifies the following predicate. خیره is an adverbial/participial quality 'staring/astounded' attached to the verb برگشت (past tense 'turned/returned'). و coordinates to the next verb بگذاشت, which is past simple 'he let/caused to be' and takes آب ('water') as its object; note the poetic/colloquial ordering places the object (آب) after the verb.
segments.s175-g138: که introduces a subordinate clause of result or report ('that/so that' / 'as'), and گفتی here functions parenthetically ('you said' / 'as you said' — a common epic narrative device). ندیدست is an older-form predication meaning 'has not been seen' or 'was not seen' (3rd singular perfect/passive sense). لشکر is the subject 'the army' and به خواب is a prepositional phrase 'into/in sleep' indicating the state 'asleep'.
segments.s175-g139: دامان here denotes the 'hem/skirt/edge (of a cloak)' and functions as the topical noun; تا means 'as far as/up to' and with به نزدیک ('to/near') marks the limit/direction. پیران is the proper name Piran (here treated grammatically as a noun meaning the person Piran), and رسید is simple past 'reached/arrived'.
segments.s175-g140: همی خواست is an older imperfective/epic-form meaning 'was about to / intended to' (the particle همی gives the ongoing or prospective sense). از identifies separation 'from/out of' and combines with تن 'body' to mark source; سرش را is the definite object 'his head' (سر + possessive clitic ش, plus object marker را placed before the verb). برید is the past of بریدن 'to cut (off)', so the whole clause means 'he was about to cut off his head from his body.'
segments.s175-g141: به خواری is an adverbial phrase 'in humiliation/with disgrace' (به + noun). پیاده modifies the action as 'on foot.' ببردش is a classical narrative form meaning 'they took/carried him' (the enclitic -ش = 'him'); کشان is a participial/manner word 'dragging/tugging' describing how he was taken. The sequence places manner phrases before and after the verb in a poetic order.
segments.s175-g142: دمان here links back to the previous action (the hem/cloak that was being tugged). و coordinates the next predicate 'پر از درد' — پر از is an adjectival phrase 'full of' (پر + از), and درد is 'pain.' چون introduces a simile 'like/as,' and بیهشان is an archaic/formal word meaning 'bereft/without them' (used comparatively). Grammatically: noun (دمان) + coordinated adjective phrase (پر از درد) + comparative phrase (چون بیهشان).
segments.s175-g143: چنین گفت is a reporting phrase 'thus he said.' کاین is a fused form of 'که این' introducing the object/content clause ('that this...'). بددل و بی‌وفا are coordinated adjectives 'ill‑hearted/inconstant and unfaithful/disloyal' describing the referred person. Grammatically, the clause sets up a reported characterization.
segments.s175-g144: گرفتار شد is a compound predication: گرفتارد (trapped/caught) plus شد (past 'became'), giving 'became trapped' or 'was caught.' در introduces the locative phrase; دم here is used poetically as 'breath' or 'gasp' (i.e., the immediate grasp/maw) and اژدها is 'dragon.' So the phrase means 'was caught in the dragon's breath/maw.'
segments.s175-g81: زمانه is the noun 'time/age' used as the subject. برو here is an adverbial/participial sense 'going on' (literary), contributing to a continuous picture. دم means a brief span 'moment' (poetic 'breath'), همی is the continuous-present marker (literary), and بشمرد is the present/continuous verbal form 'counts' (ب + شمارَد) — together: 'time, going on, keeps counting moments.'
segments.s175-g82: بیاید is a jussive/subjunctive sense 'may come' or 'let (it) come'. دمان here is the proper name Doman (the given senses don't list the name), so no listed sense fits. پیش من = 'before/in the presence of me' (پیش used as 'in the presence of'), and بگذرد is 'pass' ('to pass by'). The clause expresses a wish: 'may Doman come and pass before me.'
segments.s175-g83: زمان is 'time' as the impersonal subject. آوریدت is the archaic/poetic 2nd-person-plural imperative/optative 'bring (you)' (آورید) with the enclitic object -ت 'you' attached; here it functions roughly 'let time bring you'. کنون = 'now', and پیش من = 'before me / in my presence.'
segments.s175-g84: همان is an emphatic demonstrative 'that very / the same'; پیش again means 'before / in the presence of' (here 'in front of'). این نامدار انجمن is a noun phrase 'this renowned assembly' (این + نامدار modifying انجمن). The clause appends the specification of where/whom the same will be before.
segments.s175-g85: بدو is the dative/enclitic pronoun 'to him / unto him' (marking the indirect object). گفت is the narrative past 'said' (here introducing reported speech) — the supplied dictionary senses don't include 'said' so no listed sense fits. گیو‌ای is the vocative 'Giv, O ...' (proper name + ای as address), and سپهدار شیر are appositive epithets 'commander' and 'lion' used as honorifics for the addressee.
segments.s175-g86: سزدگر is an impersonal/fixed-phrase meaning 'it is fitting / appropriate' (literally ‘fitting-maker’); به + آب + اندر + آیی is the verbal phrase 'to enter into the water' (به ... اندر آیی = 'you go into the water'), where آیی is the present/subjunctive form 'you come/enter'. دلیر at the end is a vocative/epithet 'brave one' addressing the person. Together: 'it is fitting that you, brave one, enter the water.'
segments.s175-g87: ببینی is the present/future second-person form 'you will see' (ب- prefix + بینی). کزین = ک + زین 'from this / out of this', and پرهنر یک سوار is a noun phrase 'one skillful rider' (پرهنر = 'full of skill', سوار = 'rider'). The whole segment sets up: 'You will see from this one skillful rider ...'.
segments.s175-g88: چه is the interrogative/exclamatory 'what'; آید is the present/subjunctive 'may come / will come' or 'happen', and the separated ت + را together form ترا 'to you' (ت = 2nd-person enclitic, را = object marker), so آید ترا = 'happens to you'. بر سر is an idiom 'upon (the head of)' = 'befall', ای after سر is the vocative particle attached to 'سر' ('O [head/chief]'), and نامدار is the epithet 'renowned' addressing the addressee. Together: 'what will befall you, O renowned one.'
